items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: What force keeps a car moving in a circle around a bend?
items.correct: Friction from the road acting towards the centre of the circle — centripetal force.
items.misconception: A centrifugal force pushes the car outwards from the centre.
items.random_wrong: The car's own momentum acts as a force that drives it in a circle.
items.why: Students experience the 'outward push' in a car (a result of inertia in a rotating frame) and interpret it as a real outward force.

items.q: For a projectile launched horizontally, what affects the horizontal component of velocity during flight (ignoring air resistance)?
items.correct: Nothing — horizontal velocity stays constant; only vertical velocity changes due to gravity.
items.misconception: Gravity slows down both the vertical and horizontal motion.
items.random_wrong: The horizontal velocity increases over time because the projectile is falling.
items.why: Students treat 'gravity affects all motion' too broadly, missing that gravity acts only vertically and the components are independent.
Correct answer: 2π √(m/k). “2π √(k/m)” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Pendulum length 1 m, g = 9.81. Period ≈? (s, 2 d.p. nearest option)
items.why: Exact answer: 2.0. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
